topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the enrollment form, the ink blurring slightly under her gaze. Her hand, usually steady when signing important documents, trembled slightly as she traced the familiar script of the teacher's name: Ms. Albright. A cold knot tightened in her stomach. Her son, Leo, was starting kindergarten at Willow Creek Elementary. And Ms. Albright. The woman who had shaped Leo’s early years, the one whose patience had somehow navigated Leo’s stubborn meltdowns, was now leading his class. Eleanor leaned back in her chair, the plastic groaning beneath her. She picked up her coffee mug, the warmth doing little to soothe the sudden chill creeping up her spine. What was this arrangement? It felt like a misplaced puzzle piece, one that refused to slot into the picture she’d been constructing.

[story 2]

"You're sure about this, honey?" David asked, his voice pitched a little too high. He watched Sarah, his daughter, meticulously review the school prospectus. Sarah’s brow was furrowed in deep concentration, her lips pressed into a thin line. When she finally looked up, her eyes held a strange, distant quality. "Yes," Sarah replied, the word clipped. "It seems… convenient. Ms. Albright is teaching the second-grade class." David shifted his weight, his fingers drumming an uneven rhythm on the table edge. He felt a distinct pressure building behind his eyes, a dull ache that made focusing on the details of the curriculum feel impossible. He just wanted to shake the feeling loose, to demand an explanation that didn't arrive in the neat, predictable packages of administrative memos.

[story 3]

I found the notification tucked inside the welcome packet. It was a simple email confirmation, but the words hit me like a physical blow. Ms. Albright. My child’s teacher. I reread the sentence three times, my breath catching in my throat. I stood by the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, each drop mirroring the chaotic swirl inside my head. My jaw locked so tight I could feel the muscles strain in my neck. It wasn't a coincidence; it felt orchestrated, a strange, looping narrative I couldn't unravel. I walked over to the bookshelf, running my hand over the spines of old books, seeking some anchor in the familiar, but finding only an unsettling sense of dislocation.
